en.extract:
The golden-breasted fruiteater (Pipreola aureopectus) is a species of bird in the family Cotingidae, the cotingas. It is found in Colombia and Venezuela.


Taxonomy and systematics
The golden-breasted fruiteater was originally described as Ampelis aureo-pectus. In the 1970s at least one author treated the golden-breasted fruiteater, black-chested fruiteater (P. lubomirskii), orange-breasted fruiteater (P. jucunda), and masked fruiteater (P. pulchra) as conspecific, but retracted that treatment about a decade later. The four are now considered a superspecies. 
The golden-breasted fruiteater has three subspecies, the nominate P. a. aureopectus (Lafresnaye, 1843), P. a. decora (Bangs, 1899), and P. a. festiva (Todd, 1912). Some authors have suggested that the population of the nominate in western Colombia could be separated as a fourth subspecies.


Description
The golden-breasted fruiteater is 16.5 to 17.5 cm (6.5 to 6.9 in) long; three individuals weighed 46 to 50 g (1.6 to 1.8 oz). The sexes have very different plumage. Adult males of the nominate subspecies have dusky lores and chin on an otherwise grass-green head. Their upperparts and tail are also grass-green. Their wings have small white tips on the tertials. Their throat and the middle of their breast are bright yellow. Their lower breast is lemon-yellow that blends to green on the sides and flanks. Adult nominate females have the same head, upperparts, tail, and wings as males. Their underparts are green with yellow streaks. Both sexes have an orange-yellow iris, an orange or orange-red bill, and greenish gray legs and feet. Subspecies P. a. decora is smaller than the nominate and has a yellow band on the sides of its neck. Males of P. a. festiva have more yellow on their underparts than the nominate.


Distribution and habitat
The golden-breasted fruiteater has a disjunct distribution. The nominate subspecies is the most widespread, though found in three discrete populations. It is found on the west slope of Colombia's western Andes, from the northern central Andes of Colombia into the Andes of Venezuela east to Lara, and in the Serranía del Perijá that straddles the Colombia-Venezuela border. Subspecies P. a. decora is found in the isolated Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta of northern Colombia. P. a. festiva is found in the western Venezuelan Coastal Range of Carabobo and Aragua. The species inhabits humid, though not extremely wet, forest in the pre-montane and montane zones. In elevation it is found between 600 and 2,500 m (2,000 and 8,200 ft) in Colombia, between 800 and 2,050 m (2,600 and 6,700 ft) in the Venezuelan Andes, between 1,700 and 3,100 m (5,600 and 10,200 ft) on the Venezuelan side of the Serranía del Perijá, and between 800 and 2,050 m (2,600 and 6,700 ft) in the Venezuelan coastal mountains.


Behavior


Movement
The golden-breasted fruiteater is a year-round resident.


Feeding
The golden-breasted fruiteater is believed to feed only on fruit but details are lacking. It typically forages in pairs, mostly in the forest's mid-levels, and often joins mixed-species feeding flocks.


Breeding
The only data on the golden-breasted fruiteater's breeding biology come from northern Colombia, where the breeding season appears to span from January to June and possibly also includes September.


Vocalization
The golden-breasted fruiteater's song lasts about three seconds. It is "a high, sibiliant pseEEEeeeeeeeeaaaeeeEET tic! tic!" that rises quickly, slowly descends, and ascends at the end. It sometimes makes a shorter version, "seeeeeEEE!".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the golden-breasted fruiteater as being of Least Concern. It has a restricted range; its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered rare in Colombia and fairly common in Venezuela. It occurs in national parks in both countries.


References

The '''golden-breasted fruiteater''' ('''''Pipreola aureopectus''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Cotingidae]], the cotingas. It is found in [[Colombia]] and [[Venezuela]].<ref name=IOC15.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/cotingas/ |title=Cotingas, manakins, tityras, becards |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 15.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=March 2025 |access-date=3 March 2025 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The golden-breasted fruiteater was [[Species description|originally described]] as ''Ampelis aureo-pectus''.<ref>{{cite book | editor=F.-E. Guérin-Ménéville | date= 1843 | title=Revue zoologique | publisher=Société cuvierienne | pages=68–69 |language=French |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2271145 |access-date=August 18, 2025 }}</ref> In the 1970s at least one author treated the golden-breasted fruiteater, [[black-chested fruiteater]] (''P. lubomirskii''), [[orange-breasted fruiteater]] (''P. jucunda''), and [[masked fruiteater]] (''P. pulchra'') as [[conspecific]], but retracted that treatment about a decade later. The four are now considered a [[superspecies]].<ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 31 July 2025. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved July 31, 2025</ref> 

The golden-breasted fruiteater has three subspecies, the [[Nominate subspecies|nominate]] ''P. a. aureopectus'' ([[Frédéric de Lafresnaye|Lafresnaye]], 1843), ''P. a. decora'' ([[Outram Bangs|Bangs]], 1899), and ''P. a. festiva'' ([[W. E. Clyde Todd|Todd]], 1912).<ref name=IOC15.1/> Some authors have suggested that the population of the nominate in western Colombia could be separated as a fourth subspecies.<ref name=GBFR-BOW>Snow, D. (2020). Golden-breasted Fruiteater (''Pipreola aureopectus''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.gobfru1.01 retrieved August 18, 2025</ref>

==Description==

The golden-breasted fruiteater is {{convert|16.5|to|17.5|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long; three individuals weighed {{convert|46|to|50|g|oz|abbr=on}}. The sexes have very different plumage. Adult males of the nominate subspecies have dusky [[Lore (anatomy)|lores]] and chin on an otherwise grass-green head. Their upperparts and tail are also grass-green. Their wings have small white tips on the [[tertials]]. Their throat and the middle of their breast are bright yellow. Their lower breast is lemon-yellow that blends to green on the sides and flanks. Adult nominate females have the same head, upperparts, tail, and wings as males. Their underparts are green with yellow streaks. Both sexes have an orange-yellow iris, an orange or orange-red bill, and greenish gray legs and feet. Subspecies ''P. a. decora'' is smaller than the nominate and has a yellow band on the sides of its neck. Males of ''P. a. festiva'' have more yellow on their underparts than the nominate.<ref name=GBFR-BOW/><ref name=McMullan>{{cite book | last1 =McMullan | first1 =Miles | last2 =Donegan | first2 =Thomas M. | last3 =Quevedo | first3 =Alonso |  title = Field Guide to the Birds of Colombia | publisher = Fundación ProAves| date =2010 | location =Bogotá | page =169  |isbn =978-0-9827615-0-2 }}</ref><ref name=Hilty>{{cite book | last =Hilty | first =Steven L. | title =Birds of Venezuela | publisher =Princeton University Press | edition =second | date =2003 | location =Princeton NJ | page =651 | language =English }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The golden-breasted fruiteater has a [[disjunct distribution]]. The nominate subspecies is the most widespread, though found in three discrete populations. It is found on the west slope of Colombia's [[Cordillera Occidental (Colombia)|western Andes]], from the northern [[Cordillera Central (Colombia)|central Andes]] of Colombia into the Andes of Venezuela east to [[Lara (state)|Lara]], and in the [[Serranía del Perijá]] that straddles the Colombia-Venezuela border. Subspecies ''P. a. decora'' is found in the isolated [[Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta]] of northern Colombia. ''P. a. festiva'' is found in the western [[Venezuelan Coastal Range]] of [[Carabobo]] and [[Aragua]]. The species inhabits humid, though not extremely wet, forest in the pre-montane and montane zones. In elevation it is found between {{convert|600|and|2500|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Colombia, between {{convert|800|and|2050|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}} in the Venezuelan Andes, between {{convert|1700|and|3100|m|ft|abbr=on}} on the Venezuelan side of the Serranía del Perijá, and between {{convert|800|and|2050|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}} in the Venezuelan coastal mountains.<ref name=GBFR-BOW/><ref name=McMullan/><ref name=Hilty/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The golden-breasted fruiteater is a year-round resident.<ref name=GBFR-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The golden-breasted fruiteater is believed to feed only on fruit but details are lacking.<ref name=GBFR-BOW/> It typically forages in pairs, mostly in the forest's mid-levels, and often joins [[mixed-species feeding flock]]s.<ref name=Hilty/>

===Breeding===

The only data on the golden-breasted fruiteater's breeding biology come from northern Colombia, where the breeding season appears to span from January to June and possibly also includes September.<ref name=GBFR-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Pipreola-aureopectus |species=the golden-breasted fruiteater}}
===Vocalization===

The golden-breasted fruiteater's song lasts about three seconds. It is "a high, sibiliant ''pseEEEeeeeeeeeaaaeeeEET tic! tic!''" that rises quickly, slowly descends, and ascends at the end. It sometimes makes a shorter version, "''seeeeeEEE!''".<ref name=Hilty/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the golden-breasted fruiteater as being of Least Concern. It has a restricted range; its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered rare in Colombia and fairly common in Venezuela.<ref name=McMullan/><ref name=Hilty/> It occurs in national parks in both countries.<ref name=GBFR-BOW/>
